paragraphs.text: Having spoken of Adam’s expulsion from the garden of Eden, of the Cherubim, and of the edge of the sword that guarded paradise, Moses turns to recount the birth of Cain and Abel, the offering of their sacrifices, and says: “And Adam knew Eve… and she bore Cain and said, I have gotten a man” (Gen 4:1) — not from Adam who had known her, but from the Lord who had formed the fruit in her womb. “And she again bore… Abel. And Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground” (Gen 4:2) — that is, when they had come of age, the one became a shepherd and the other a tiller.

“And it came to pass… that Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering unto God, and Abel… brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof” (Gen 4:3–4). Abel brought his offering with choice; Cain brought without choice. Abel chose and offered the firstborn and the fat; Cain offered either the ears of grain, or together with the ears, the fruits that were at that time. Although his offering was poorer than his brother’s, if he had brought it not with neglect, his offering too would have been pleasing, just as his brother’s was. But since, when both brought their sacrifices together — one his sheep from his flocks, and the other the fruits of the earth — Cain at the very beginning of the sacrifice showed his negligence, God did not will to receive his offering, that He might teach him how it should be offered. Cain had oxen and calves; he had no lack of beasts or birds to offer in sacrifice, but he did not bring them on the day when the firstfruits of the earth ought to be offered. And could he have lacked grain to bring good ears in sacrifice, or fruits of trees from which to choose the best? But he did not do this, though it was easy (possible); he did not trouble to choose good ears or better fruits. In the soul of him who offered the sacrifice there was no love for the One who receives the offerings. And since he offered the sacrifice with neglect, God rejected it, so that Cain might not think that his negligence was unknown to God, or that gifts were more pleasing to Him than the offerers themselves. God rejected Cain’s offering both for what had been done by him, and for what he was about to do, because Cain was disobedient to his parents, cruel to his brother, and irreverent before God. So Abel’s offering was accepted because of Abel’s zeal, and Cain’s rejected because of Cain’s negligence.

“Cain was grieved” — not that his offering had been rejected, for by a chosen offering he could have appeased the One angered by a negligent offering. “And his countenance fell” (Gen 4:5) — but again not because he had been rejected, for he could have offered prayer to God. But whether or not his chosen offering would have been pleasing, if he had brought it — he had already shown what he wished. Whether or not God would have been appeased by his prayers — Cain had already shown what he sought. If, in place of the negligent offering rejected by God, he did not bring a chosen offering, and did not by prayer make amends for the contempt with which he had offered sacrifice to God, then it is plain that the acceptance of his brother’s sacrifice grieved him. He was grieved by the fire which came down from heaven, by which one offering was marked off from the other. “His countenance fell,” because the rejection of his offering put him in fear of being mocked by his parents and sisters, who saw that fire had touched his offering, but it remained unaccepted.

“And the Lord God said unto Cain: Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen?” (Gen 4:6). Instead of being filled with wrath, thou shouldest be filled with contrition; instead of having thy face downcast, thou shouldest pour out tears from thine eyes. “If thou doest well, hast thou not brought?” — see, Cain’s offering was rejected not because it was small, but because it was offered with negligence and irreverence — “If thou doest well, hast thou not brought?” — though thou hadst brought nothing, together with thy brother’s chosen offering thy own offering too would have been accepted, even if it seemed unacceptable. “And if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door.” “And unto thee Abel shall turn, and shall hearken to thee, and shall go with thee into the field.” “But thou shalt be lord over sin” (Gen 4:7–8) — that is, it is in thy power not to commit it. But Cain, though God had promised that, if he should do well, his offering would be accepted as well-pleasing — to the One to whom he had earlier offered a sacrifice of negligence, now offers in sacrifice also a murder.

“And Cain said unto Abel his brother: Let us go into the field” (Gen 4:8). He says “let us go into the field” either because they lived on a mountain within the bounds of paradise and Cain brings him down from there to the plain, or because Abel was tending his flock on a mountain, and Cain leads him thence to the plain, where, because of the abundance of grain-stalks and the softness of the soil, it would be more convenient for him to commit the murder and hide the slain in the earth. For Scripture says: “And it came to pass, when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him” (Gen 4:8).

Cain, having slain his brother, falsely assured his parents that Abel had been brought into paradise because he had pleased God, and said that the bringing of him into paradise was witnessed by the pleasing of Abel’s sacrifice — for keeping the commandment had brought Adam into paradise, just as transgressing it had cast him out of paradise. When Cain thought he had deceived his parents and that there was no one to avenge Abel, then God appears to Cain and says: “Where is Abel thy brother?” (Gen 4:9). God appears to him without wrath, so that, if he repents, the prayer spoken by his lips might wipe away the sin of murder committed by his hand; but if he does not repent, a heavy punishment was appointed him, such as the evil deed deserved. But Cain, instead of repentance, is filled with indignation, and to the All-Knowing One, who had questioned him about his brother in order to draw Cain to Himself, answers in wrath: “I know not: am I my brother’s keeper?” (Gen 4:9). God continues: “What hast thou done?” (Gen 4:10). If thou knowest not where Abel is, because thou art not his keeper, then to Him who asks thee about what thou hast done, tell Him what thou hast done, so that He need not ask others about thee. Say, “What hast thou done?” If the One who asks thee did not know what was done by thee, He would not ask thee about thy deed. But since Cain would not say even this — what was done by him — then God’s knowledge is shown, and God, distinguishing the transgression, says: “The voice of thy brother’s blood crieth unto me from the ground” (Gen 4:10). What wilt thou say, Cain? Shall Justice avenge or not avenge the blood that cries to Him? Was Justice not delaying that thou mightest repent? Was He not hiding His knowledge and questioning thee as one ignorant, that thou shouldest confess thy evil deed? But since to do good was not pleasing to thee, as Justice was prompting, and thou didst rush into the sin from which Justice had warned thee beforehand, “cursed art thou upon the earth,” because thou hast caused grief to Adam and Eve, the forefathers of all the earth; “cursed art thou upon the earth” (Gen 4:11), because thou hast opened to all the earth the gates of Sheol (hell). “When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength,” because thou aloneness wishedst to enjoy its produce — “a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth” (Gen 4:12), for thou hast walked upon it with pride and arrogance.

Since this curse was at once fulfilled in deed, he who before had spoken with pride, “I am not my brother’s keeper,” after the pronouncement of the curse, brought down from the height of his pride, began at once to groan and tremble. He says: “My iniquity is too great to be forgiven” (Gen 4:13). But this confession was not accepted, because Cain did not bring it forth at the time when he was being questioned, but brought it forth against his will, speaking it only when it was already appointed to him: “a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be.” And Cain, as though unwilling to appease Justice by his prayers, instead of beseeching God’s long-suffering, again (with defiance) — whether from fear or from cunning intent — says: “Behold, thou drivest me from the face of the ground” — thou hast cursed me from the face of the ground; and I “from thy face shall be hid” — that is, I cannot stand before Thee, having shown myself bold before Thy face, saying, “I am not my brother’s keeper.” “And I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth: and it shall come to pass, that every one that findeth me shall slay me” (Gen 4:14). Dost thou wish death for thyself, Cain, or fearest thou death? But if thou diest, how shall God’s appointment over thee be fulfilled? And if life is dear to thee even amid such miseries, how much more desirable was it to Abel, who was far from thy miseries.

Some say that Cain here besought deliverance from death; others assert that he begged for the hastening of death — whereupon God said to him: “It shall not be so with thee, as with murderers after thee.” Murderers after Cain shall be put to death as soon as they are discovered, but “whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold” (Gen 4:15). Since he had asked for death for himself, that men should not laugh at his humiliation, for seven generations they shall see his humiliation, and only then shall he die.

But what some say cannot be accepted, namely that with Cain there perished seven generations of his descendants. If we suppose that Cain’s descendants were destroyed by the Flood, then the Flood destroyed only the seventh generation. And if one generation perished with Cain, why do they say that seven generations perished with Cain? Moreover, those who assert this cannot prove that the Flood was in the seventh generation of Cain. For Scripture says that of Cain was born Enoch, of Enoch was born Irad (Gaidad), of Irad was born Mehujael (Maleleel), of Mehujael was born Methushael, of Methushael was born Lamech, and of Lamech was born Jabal, and “he was the father of such as dwell in tents, and have cattle” (Gen 4:20). Of course these tent-dwelling cattle-keepers did not keep virginity in their tents, especially since Scripture says, “all flesh had corrupted its way upon the earth” (Gen 6:12). And if from Cain to the sons of those who dwelt in tents and were cattle-keepers there passed nine generations, and the Flood had not yet come, how can one agree with those who assert that with Cain there perished seven generations — when, as we have said, nine generations had passed, and the Flood had not yet begun? Therefore it is rightly said that Cain’s shame lasted until the seventh generation, even though Cain had asked that death might deliver him from that shame on the very first day. That Cain lived until the seventh generation is clear first from the fact that this was the determination of God’s judgment, and second is confirmed by the lengths of human life in the earliest generations. If Cain’s father, Adam, lived until the ninth generation, that is until the sons of Lamech, and died in the fifty-sixth year of Lamech’s life, it is no wonder that Cain too lived to the seventh generation.

Though Cain had asked to be delivered from his shame, he was not only not delivered, as he had wished, but beyond his expectation, in addition to the punishment already appointed him, a sign was added. For it is said: “And the Lord God set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him” (Gen 4:15). By those who would find Cain we must understand the descendants of Seth, who had a motive to avenge the blood of their uncle Abel, and who shunned Cain both because of his shame and his groaning, and entered into no marriage unions with his tribe; yet, because there was a sign upon Cain, they did not dare to kill him.

When Cain had borne his punishment, and besides the punishment a sign had been added (the cause of which we have stated, but we are silent — as unnecessary — about what it consisted in), then Scripture says of Cain: “And Cain went out from the presence of the Lord, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden” (Gen 4:16). Cain withdrew from his parents and brothers, for he saw that they would not enter into marriage with him. The land was called Nod (Naid or Nod) because it too trembled and groaned. It is likely that, by the word of God, it had fallen under the other curse: “When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength” (Gen 4:12). After this “Cain knew his wife, and she conceived and bore Enoch. And he was the builder of a city, and called the city after the name of his son, Enoch” (Gen 4:17). He did this so that the city should not also be called Nod, that is, “city of trembling.” “And to Enoch was born Irad (Gaidad), and Irad begat Mehujael (Maleleel), and Mehujael begat Methushael, and Methushael begat Lamech. And Lamech took unto him two wives… and Adah bore Jabal: he was the father of such as dwell in tents, and have cattle. And his brother’s name was Jubal: he was the father of all such as handle the harp and organ. And Zillah… bore Tubal-cain (Thobel): an instructor of every artificer in brass and iron: and the sister of Tubal-cain was Naamah. And Lamech said unto his wives… Hear my voice… for I have slain a man for my wound, and a young man for my hurt. If Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and sevenfold” (Gen 4:18–24). Some, explaining Lamech’s words to his wives, say that the wives were of the tribe of Seth and counseled Lamech to become pious. But he says to them: “What see ye in me that is foul and like what was done by my father Cain? Have I slain a man, for a wound to me, as Cain did, or, just as Cain slew the youth Abel, striking him on the cheek, have I slain a youth, for a hurt to me? If I have acted like Cain, then Cain was punished sevenfold, but I appoint for myself that I be punished seventy times sevenfold.”

Others, holding that the vengeance for Cain should extend to the seventh generation, and basing themselves on the words, “All flesh had corrupted its way” (Gen 6:12), say that Lamech was wicked. And since his wives saw that the succession of their line was being broken off, because those born to them were not male but female (according to the saying, “when men began to multiply on the face of the earth, and daughters were born unto them” [Gen 6:1]) — such a diminution in the multiplication of their kind brought them into fear, suggesting the thought that the judgment pronounced against Cain and the seven generations of his descendants was overtaking them. Therefore Lamech encourages his wives with his cunning answer and says: “I have slain a man for my wound, and a youth for my hurt” (Gen 4:23). If God prolonged Cain’s punishment so that with him seven generations perished, then to me, who have slain two, He shall prolong it still more, so that with me must perish the seventy generations taken seven times; we shall die, and having tasted the cup of death, we shall be freed from the punishment which for my sake shall extend to the seventy-times-sevenfold generation.

Others say that Lamech, being cunning and crafty, seeing that his line was diminishing while the descendants of Seth would not consent to enter into unions with them because of the shame lying upon their forefather Cain — so that the earth should not remain untilled for lack of cultivators, and that their race should not entirely disappear, Lamech zealously sought the good of his tribe and killed Cain and one of his sons who most resembled his father, so that the son’s likeness to the father might not lay a shameful stain upon their whole tribe. So, having killed Cain and as it were removed the barrier that had divided the lines of Cain and Seth and prevented them from entering into family connections with each other, Lamech as if in secret says to his wives: “A man and a youth have been slain; adorn your daughters for the sons of Seth, for the murders I have committed and the beauty and adornment of your daughters will bring it about that even those who in the six generations past were unwilling to have such unions with us will now agree to enter into marriage unions with us.”

And Lamech’s wives adorn their daughters for the sons of Seth. Jabal pleases them at feasts with the meats of animals. Jubal captivates them with the sweet sounds of his harp. And the sons of Seth rush to them, forget the beautiful covenant bequeathed to them by their father, leave their dwellings, which were higher than the dwellings of Cain’s tribe. By such cunning Lamech brought confusion upon both lines, reasoning thus: if God shall be merciful to the line of Seth, which has entered into union with us, and preserve it from destruction, He will also show mercy to us; and through this we shall be delivered from punishment for murder for the sake of those who are innocent of murder and have entered into marriage unions with us.
